question: What is the value of x?  (I) x = 10.  (II) x is a positive even number.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: I alone fixes x = 10. II alone allows 2, 4, 6, … — not unique. So I alone is sufficient.
trick: I gives one value; II gives many.

question: What is the value of y?  (I) y is a multiple of 3.  (II) y = 9.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: II alone fixes y = 9. I alone allows 3, 6, 9, … — not unique. So II alone is sufficient.
trick: II gives one value; I gives many.

question: What is the value of x?  (I) x + y = 20.  (II) y = 8.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: I alone has two unknowns; II alone says nothing about x. Together x = 20 − 8 = 12.

question: What is the value of x?  (I) x > 5.  (II) x < 20.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: Each gives a range; together 5 < x < 20 still allows many values, so not sufficient.

question: What is the area of a square?  (I) Its side is 6 cm.  (II) It is a square.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: I alone gives area = 6² = 36. II merely restates the shape, adding nothing.
trick: Side fixes the area; II is filler.
type: Trap: looks like C, actually A

question: What is Ram's age?  (I) Ram is older than Sita.  (II) Ram is 25 years old.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: II alone gives 25. I alone only compares him to Sita — not a value.
trick: II is a number; I is a comparison.

question: What is the perimeter of a rectangle?  (I) Its length is 10.  (II) Its breadth is 4.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: Perimeter needs both length and breadth: neither alone works, together P = 2(10+4) = 28.

question: What is the value of n?  (I) n is an integer.  (II) n² = 16.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: II gives n = 4 or −4; I only says integer. Together n is still 4 or −4 — two values, not unique.

question: Is the number even?  (I) It ends in 0.  (II) It is greater than 100.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: I alone: ending in 0 means it is even — a definite YES. II alone: could be odd or even.

question: What is the speed of a car?  (I) It travelled a long way.  (II) It covered 120 km in 2 hours.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: II alone gives speed = 120 ÷ 2 = 60 km/h. I alone is vague.

question: What is the value of x?  (I) 2x + y = 14.  (II) y = 4.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: I alone has two unknowns; II gives y only. Together 2x + 4 = 14 → x = 5.

question: What is the value of x?  (I) x is a prime number.  (II) x is less than 10.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: Together, primes below 10 are 2, 3, 5, 7 — several values, so not unique.

question: What is the cost of one pen?  (I) 5 pens cost ₹50.  (II) The pens are blue.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: I alone gives 50 ÷ 5 = ₹10 per pen. II (colour) is irrelevant.

question: What is the value of n?  (I) n is a two-digit number.  (II) n = 42.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: II alone fixes n = 42. I alone allows any two-digit number.

question: What is the value of x?  (I) x = y + 3.  (II) y = 7.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: I relates x to y; II gives y. Together x = 7 + 3 = 10.

question: What is the value of x?  (I) x ≠ 0.  (II) x² > 1.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: Together, x² > 1 means x > 1 or x < −1 — infinitely many values, not unique.

question: How many students are in the class?  (I) There are exactly 30 students.  (II) There are more than 20 students.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: I alone gives 30. II alone only bounds the number below.
trick: I is exact; II is a bound.

question: What is the discount percent?  (I) The original price was high.  (II) An item marked ₹200 sold for ₹150.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: II alone: discount = 50/200 = 25%. I alone is vague.

question: What is the two-digit number?  (I) Its tens digit is 4.  (II) Its units digit is 7.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: I alone gives 4_, II alone gives _7; together the number is 47.

question: What is the value of x?  (I) xy = 12.  (II) x + y = 7.
options: Even both together are not sufficient
solution: Together x and y are 3 and 4, but which is x is not fixed — x could be 3 or 4, so not unique.
